coredata.dc:title: A facile decoration of anatase Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposite with graphene quantum dots: Synthesis, characterization, and photocatalytic activity

Nojavan, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: A combination of electron-rich graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposites may develop an efficient electron transfer for enhanced photocatalytic activity. In this report, a facile decoration of GQDs with maltose precursor was synthesized and loaded onto magnetic anatase TiO2 nanocomposites under hydrothermal methods. The as-synthetized magnetic TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite resulted in a specific surface area of 38.00 m2/g and a total pore volume of 0.186 cm3/g. The HRTEM images showed a lattice plane distance of 0.350 nm related to the interplanar spacing of the anatase TiO2 (101) plane and that of 0.299 nm observed for the in-plane lattice part of GQDs. The effects of magnetic loading ratio and GQDs loading onto TiO2, pH, photocatalyst dosage, and methylene blue (MB) concentration were thoroughly evaluated to find the optimum conditions of mineralization MB for getting the highest photocatalytic efficiency. The removal efficiency of around 86.08 ± 3.62% was obtained at pH11, photocatalytic dose 400 mg/100 mL, and MB concentration 10 mg/L. Moreover, the photogenerated electron transfers and MB degradation mechanism by the resulting Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS under irradiation of UVA light are proposed. The as-synthesized material improved meaningfully greater photocatalytic efficiency for degrading MB under UVA light irradiation than merely pure anatase TiO2. Also, the predominant mechanism of MB degradation was direct oxidative decomposition through the photogenerated holes. The photocatalytic destruction of MB complied with the apparent first-order models under UVA light irradiation.

head.ce:title: A facile decoration of anatase Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposite with graphene quantum dots: Synthesis, characterization, and photocatalytic activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A combination of electron-rich graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposites may develop an efficient electron transfer for enhanced photocatalytic activity. In this report, a facile decoration of GQDs with maltose precursor was synthesized and loaded onto magnetic anatase TiO2 nanocomposites under hydrothermal methods. The as-synthetized magnetic TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite resulted in a specific surface area of 38.00 m2/g and a total pore volume of 0.186 cm3/g. The HRTEM images showed a lattice plane distance of 0.350 nm related to the interplanar spacing of the anatase TiO2 (101) plane and that of 0.299 nm observed for the in-plane lattice part of GQDs. The effects of magnetic loading ratio and GQDs loading onto TiO2, pH, photocatalyst dosage, and methylene blue (MB) concentration were thoroughly evaluated to find the optimum conditions of mineralization MB for getting the highest photocatalytic efficiency. The removal efficiency of around 86.08 ± 3.62% was obtained at pH11, photocatalytic dose 400 mg/100 mL, and MB concentration 10 mg/L. Moreover, the photogenerated electron transfers and MB degradation mechanism by the resulting Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS under irradiation of UVA light are proposed. The as-synthesized material improved meaningfully greater photocatalytic efficiency for degrading MB under UVA light irradiation than merely pure anatase TiO2. Also, the predominant mechanism of MB degradation was direct oxidative decomposition through the photogenerated holes. The photocatalytic destruction of MB complied with the apparent first-order models under UVA light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: The textile dyes are believed to be highly toxic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic agents [1], and also prevent light penetration through water [2] and lead to a reduction of photosynthetic activity [3] that cause dissolved oxygen levels to drop in water bodies and limit downstream beneficial uses such as agriculture, recreation, drinking water supply, and irrigation [4]. Therefore, the strategic technology could revolutionize treatment systems to be applied for pollution removal from the textile dyes industry for sustainable development to future generations.

Photocatalytic phenomena have been known for about four decades, but this technology is being developed very rapidly for various applications such as renewable energy production, environmental safety, water treatment, and antibacterial processes [5]. In recent days, many materials develop as photocatalyst which includes metal oxides, metal–organic frameworks, plasmonic hybrids, and metal–semiconductor hybrid nanostructures, but TiO2 is the most considerable owing to its low cost, high chemical and biochemical stability, and efficient photoactivity [6]. Though, there are two limitations to TiO2 as a photocatalyst. First, the band gaps of rutile and anatase forms of TiO2 were stated to be 3.0 and 3.20 eV, respectively, which decreases the photon absorption of semiconductors because the photon energies of light must be greater than the energy of bandgap semiconductor [7]. The second disadvantage of TiO2 is related to its recovery and reuse for potential industrial applications. On the other hand, the collection of dispersed TiO2 particles in the wastewater is difficult. It can be solved with coating magnetization. Therefore, the surface coating of TiO2 onto a magnetic core could be an effective strategy to develop a recoverable composite photocatalyst, which can be collected by the external magnetic fields to have good photocatalytic activity and reusability. Many studies have been synthesized core–shell TiO2/Fe3O4 to achieve super magnetism TiO2 [8–11]. Another useful strategy, which has been considered for its special properties nowadays, is carbon-based quantum dots-modified photocatalyst composite [12]. It shows photoluminescence properties, high solubility in water environments, low toxicity, and high resistance to photobleaching [13]. In this report, the graphene quantum dots, which compose of both single- and multi-layers of small fragments of graphene, are used as an alternative material to improve the surface chemistry of anatase TiO2 photocatalyst. The structure modification of anatase TiO2 is used to decrease electron-hole recombination due to its low electron mobility, oxygen vacancy defects in surface TiO2, and poor structural stability against UV light. In recent years, the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/TiO2 (commercial Degussa P25) composite showed a better photocatalytic activity performance for photooxidation of methylene blue (MB) [12] and degradation of Rhodamine [14] under UV light. A study revealed that the nitrogen/sulfur co-doped GQDs (S/N-GQDs)/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited high photocatalytic activities in methylene blue (MB) degradation [15]. In another work, Fe3O4/SiO2/TiO2 composites were prepared using a hetero-agglomeration method that exhibited higher photodegradation efficiency for methylene blue (MB) dye removal in water compared with pure TiO2 [8]. The photocatalytic activity of hybrid material based on natural zeolite modified with nitrogen-doped TiO2 photocatalyst under UV irradiation was used for the degradation of reactive yellow 125 dye solution [16].

The present paper as whole grants a completely new set of magnetically anatase TiO2/Fe3O4 nanocomposite decorated with GQDs, which has a noticeable difference in the crystalline quality and robust photocatalyst material for the treatment of pollutants as previously aforementioned. The aim was to prepare a magnetically Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite photocatalyst through hydrothermal routes. Our preliminary study attempted to synthesize Fe3O4 coated on the anatase TiO2 by adjusting the iron oxide amount in Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposite of which was decorated finally by the various amount of GQDs in a new approach. The chemical composition, surface morphology, crystalline behavior, and magnetic characteristic of as-synthesized materials were examined by numerous techniques, such as X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetry analysis (TGA), BET N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, the point of zero charges (pHzpc), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), field-emission scanning microscopy (FESEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and magnetic analysis. The GQDs-coated magnetic porous TiO2 as a photocatalyst was used for the removal of model compounds MB dye. The photocatalytic activity and mechanism of the nanocomposite for MB oxidation were tested and discussed.

Maltose, iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), iron (III) chloride anhydrous (FeCl3), ammonia solutions (NH3, 25 wt%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 wt%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), titanium(IV) tetraisopropoxide (Ti(OCH(CH3)2)4, TTIP) and anhydrous 2-propanol were provided by Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany. All reagents employed in this work were of analytical purity and were utilized as received without any further purification. Also, double-distilled water (DD H2O) was consumed for the synthesis of the materials and stock solutions.

The magnetic particles were prepared via the coprecipitation method from mixed ferric and ferrous salts solution in basic medium producing precipitation of the resultant hydroxides [17]. Briefly, 0.73 g FeCl3 was dissolved in DD water in a three-necked flask and maintained under an inert gas (nitrogen atmosphere) with vigorous stirring at ambient temperature. The mixture was heated to 80 °C and agitated on a magnetic stirrer. Then, 0.68 g FeSO4·7H2O was slowly added to the aforementioned solution comprising Fe (III), which was agitated vigorously for an additional 30 min. With a pH meter, the solution of ammonia was poured dropwise into the above mixture solution until the pH reached 10. Then, the reaction was adopted to proceed for 30 min under vigorous mixing conditions to attain iron oxyhydroxides [FeO(OH)]. An external magnet separated the black precipitations, and then samples were rinsed with DD water many times until the neutral pH value of 7.0. Finally, the coal-black products were air-dried at room temperature to produce the oxide formula of magnetite Fe3O4. Then, the sample was kept in a glass vial under an inert atmosphere to avoid further oxidation after the preparation process.

Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticle was fabricated by the sol–gel method [9] utilizing HCl as a catalyst for hydrolysis of TTIP in anhydrous 2-propanol as a solvent. In a typical synthesis, 1 g Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in a 39 mL anhydrous 2-propanol. The mixture was sonicated for 5 min to confirm homogeneity. Then, 1 mL of 0.1 M HCl was added to the above suspension under stirring. Afterward, 10 mL of 1.25 M TTIP dissolved in anhydrous 2-propanol was poured slowly dropwise to the above mixture under constant stirring at room temperature. The mixture was heated to 40 °C in a water bath under stirring to hydrolyze TTIP in the presence of acid to form TiO2. Finally, the suspension of titanium sol and the Fe3O4 nanoparticles was ultrasonicated for another 30 min. The solvent was then evaporated using rotary evaporation under vacuum at 40 °C overnight and rinsed with ethanol and DD water until pH 7. The products were dried at 60 °C and then calcinated at 450 °C for 3 h at 5 °C min−1 heating rate under air to procedure magnetic TiO2 crystalline in the form of anatase phase. Subsequently, obtained photocatalysts were prepared with different Fe3O4 loading onto TiO2 at weight ratios of 0.125: 1, 0.25: 1, 0.5: 1, and 1: 1 and are identified as FT0.125, FT0.25, FT0.5, and FT1, respectively. The bare TiO2 was prepared by the same procedure without adding dispersant Fe3O4 and use as a reference.

The GQDS were prepared by a modified hydrothermal routine [18] using maltose, HCl, and DD water. In this reaction, maltose acts as a carbon precursor and HCl act as a catalyst. Typically, 8 mmol of maltose was dissolved in 10 mL DD water, followed by the addition of 10 mL of concentrated HCl. After stirring the solution of maltose and HCl, the mixture was transferred in a 100-ml Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal autoclave, sealed, and heated up to 200 °C for 3 h. The dispersant GQDS was filtered through an ashless grade 42 quantitative filter paper (5 μm particle retention). The resulting residue was neutralized with NaOH until pH reached 7, rinsed with DD water, air-dried, and stored at 25 °C for future characterization. Under the hydrothermal condition, maltose carbon source dehydrated inter-and intra-molecularly in aqueous media and in the presence of HCl catalyst to form a honeycomb carbon matrix, and polymerization and carbonization (graphitization) reaction occurred at high temperature as presented in Fig. 1 .

The Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS composite was prepared using an alteration method that existed in the literature [12]. Briefly, 1 g of Fe3O4/TiO2 (FT0.5) composites was poured into 40 mL DD water and 20 mL methanol under vigorous stirring. The resulting suspensions were sonicated for 15 min. Then, 1 mL of GQDS suspension (1 mg/ml) was transferred into the aforementioned suspension. The above mixture was then stirred for 2 h at ambient temperature to get a homogenous and stable suspension. The suspension contents were loaded in a 150-ml Teflon-lined stainless steel solvo-hydrothermal autoclave reactor and heated to 130 °C for 4 h. Finally, the resulting composite was separated by an external magnetic bar, rinsed with DD water for 5 min, 5 times, and ethanol for 5 min, 5 times, air-dried, and stored at 25 °C for future use. The above-mentioned route refers to Fe3O4/TiO2 loaded with 0.1 wt% GQDs. With the addition of QDs precursors to Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticles, the surface hydroxyl (OH) group of TiO2 reacts with the carboxyl groups (COOH) on the GQDs nanosheets and introduced a development of TiOC bonds as displays in Fig. 1. This is to be emphasized that the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs mentioned here, all corresponded to 0.1 wt% GQDs on Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticles (weight ratio of 1: 0.1). To explore the potential effect of GQDs amount on the photocatalytic reactivity of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposites, Fe3O4/TiO2 loaded by GQDs with various mounts (weight ratio of 1: 0, 1: 0.1, 1: 0.5, 1: 1.0, and 0: 1.0) were also prepared with the aforementioned method (MethodsX).

The mineralogical composition of as-synthesized samples was done using X-ray diffraction (X’Pert PW1730, Philips, Netherlands) with a nickel filtrated CuKα radiation and a setting of 40 kV and 40 mA. The data was collected at 2θ ranging from 5 to 70° with a step size of 0.05°/s. To compare the crystal size of all synthesized materials, Scherrer's formula can be employed to measure the crystallite size from XRD data based on the following equation:
(1)
D =
k λ
β c o s θ
where D is the crystal thickness (nm), λ the X-ray radiation with Cu Kα(wavelength (λ) = 0.154 nm), k the Scherrer constant for spherical particle shape (0.89), β the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) peak intensity, and θ the Bragg’s angle. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of samples was carried out using a Q600 SDT-TA instrument analyzer (USA). The loss in weight was determined continuously as it was maintained at a defined temperature. Approximately, about 6 mg weight of the sample was placed in alumina crucibles and heated from room temperature to 800 °C at a 10 °Cm min−1 rate under argon flow. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen (N2) adsorption/desorption isotherm at 77 K was examined by the use of computer-controlled volumetric equipment (BET, Bel Japan, Inc.). The surface functional groups were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, FTIR1650, Japan) with the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Specs, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) was performed by a K-alpha and equipped with an aluminum Kα radiation source (1486.6 eV Al Kα) and the ultra-high vacuum chamber as being able to attain a pressure of less than 10−9 Torr. The as-synthesized samples' surface morphology was performed by a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SIGMA VP Zeiss, Germany), equipped with an X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX Instrument, UK), which was applied to measure the elemental compositions of the samples. The specimens were sputter-coated with gold before working in the FESM. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image analysis and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) layer analysis were attained by a Zeiss-EM10C microscope operated at 300 kV, for which the specimen preparation was done by depositing a drop of ethanol suspension onto the TEM Cu grid and air-dried. The saturation magnetization and magnetic hysteresis loops were estimated in the air at ambient temperature by a Lakeshore 7410 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, USA). The absorption spectrum of the samples was quantified using a V-650 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, Jasco, Hachioji, Tokyo, Japan) with a wavelength ranging from 200 to 1000 nm. The Tauc plot is established on the postulation that the photon energy-dependent absorption coefficient α(E) is stated by the following expression [19]:
(2)
α h v
n
= B
h v -
E g
where α is the magnitude of the absorption coefficient of the semiconductor, ν the photon’s frequency, h the Planck’s constant, n the parameter of the electron transition mode, which sets 2 for the direct transition bandgap, Eg the bandgap energy, and B a constant. The pH at the point of zero charges (pHzpc) of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposite was measured to estimate the surface charge of the sample at the pHzpc. The initial pH of 10 mL of 0.01 M NaCl solution was adjusted between 3 and 11 by using either HCl or NaOH solution. The NaCl solution was bubbled with nitrogen gas (N2) to remove the dissolved carbon dioxide at room temperature to get a stable initial pH reading. Then, about 0.3 g of the sample was added to the aforementioned solution. After a contact time of 48 h, the zeta potential values were recorded by a ZEN3600 Malvern instrument (Zetasizer Nano instrument, UK).

The photodegradation experimental runs were carried out in a batch reactor. A 500-mL cylindrical tube reactor was operated for the harvesting of 100 mL MB solution comprising a fixed quantity of photocatalyst. The MB in aqueous photocatalyst suspension was first purged with N2 gas under atmospheric pressure, equipped with a magnetic stir bar, at room temperature for about 30 min to confirm the adsorption–desorption equilibrium under a dark situation. The degradation efficiencies of MB dye solution were estimated to highlight the effect of TiO2 coating (Fe to Ti ratio of 1: 1, 1: 2, 1: 4, and 1: 8) on the photocatalyst structure for improving the photocatalytic activity of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS while reaction temperature, photocatalyst dose, and pH were kept constant. The Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalytic processes were then performed to destroy MB under different solution pH conditions (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), photocatalyst dose (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 mg/100 mL), and initial dye solution (5, 10, 15, and 20 mg/L) to validate the effect of the process performance on photocatalytic activity. The tube reactor was irradiated by employing a low-pressure mercury-vapor lamp as a UVA irradiation source (Philips, 9 W, Nederlands) located within an axial situation inside a quartz sleeve. The photocatalytic removal efficiency of dye using Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites was computed by:

Thus, MB concentration (C t) versus time is plotted by nonlinear regression analysis, the slope of which is the apparent first-order rate constant (kapp , min−1).
The as-synthesized products characterized and confirmed instrumentally using several techniques are discussed in the following section as well as in Supplementary Materials.

Fig. 2 a displays the phase components of TiO2, Fe3O4, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanoparticles. Fig. 2a shows the diffraction peak of the synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles and indicates the anatase form of TiO2 because there are two characteristic peaks at 2θ = 25 and 38 (JCPDS no. 21-1272) [20]. Fig. 2a shows the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 which is pure [17], with an inverse cubic spinel structure [21]. This figure shows seven characteristic peak intensities for Fe3O4 nanoparticles (2θ = 18.2, 30.1, 35.5, 43.3, 53.4, 57.2 and 62.5). Though, the observed peaks are in accordance with the standard database in the JCPDS file (PDF No. 65-3107) and diffraction peaks conforming to the indices with their (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) obtained from XRD data [22]. Fig. 2a displays the XRD pattern of GQDS. The GQDS have broad peaks at 002 [18], which centered around 2θ = 21.5° [23], indicating the small size of GQDS. The interlayer distance was 0.407 nm, which is broader than graphite [24] and was in accordance with reported values (0.343 to 0.481 nm) of GQDS prepared by the carbonization of glucose [25]. As a result, both interlayer spacing and broad peak existed in as-synthesized GQDS. Fig. 2a displays the XRD pattern of Fe3O4/TiO2 composite. When Fe3O4 was loaded on TiO2, the intensity of diffraction peaks reduced, but the two crystal structures of Fe3O4 and TiO2 nanoparticles coexisted in the Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposite and were the same of bare nanoparticles, indicating anatase form and magnetization process did not cause the change of crystal phase structure. Fig. 2a displays the XRD pattern of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS which TiO2 and GQDS peaks overlay each other but peaks of anatase and magnetite are visible, indicating successful synthesis of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS.

According to Scherrer's equation, the crystallite sizes of bare forms of anatase TiO2, Fe3O4, and GQDS and composite materials were computed at 2θ = 25.42, 35.5, and 21.5. The diffraction maxima applied were for lattice spacing of the anatase TiO2 phase for the (101) plane, (311) plane in Fe3O4 and (002) plane in GQDS. The crystallite sizes of anatase TiO2 were about 26.14, 29.18, and 26.97 nm in bare TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites, respectively. Also, the crystallite sizes of Fe3O4 were about 13.01, 20.34, and 19.84 nm for bare Fe3O4, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites, respectively. Correspondingly, the crystallite sizes of GQDS were about 0.82 and 0.88 nm in bare GQDS nanoparticles and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites respectively. The computed average crystalline sizes reveal that the highest crystalline size value corresponded to the Fe3O4/TiO2 sample and the lowest value corresponded to the bare samples. Moreover, the major peak of Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticles shows the broader peak width and weaker peak intensity compared with bare TiO2, which indicates greater crystallite size due to the formation of layered TiO2 nanoparticles in the bulk crystal of porous nanostructure [26]. Besides, Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposite reveals a highly similar XRD pattern to that of bare GQDS nanoparticles, which demonstrates that the magnetic TiO2 decorated with GQDs was no effect on their crystal structure [27].

The results of the thermogravimetric analysis of nanocomposite stability for TiO2, Fe3O4, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanoparticles are illustrated in Fig. 2b. The TGA graph patterns for TiO2, Fe3O4, and Fe3O4/TiO2 can roughly be divided into three discrete and continuous intervals; 25–200, 200–400, and 400–800 °C. Results show that the temperature at which water molecules are lost within a temperature range of 40–150 °C due to the removal of H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface. TGA diagram of TiO2, Fe3O4, (about 1%), and Fe3O4/TiO2 (about 3%) have negligible loss weight and are thermally stable but GQDS sample present impressive weight loss and are not thermal stable. After that, the TGA curves of the sample carry on to lose the weight up to 3% for TiO2, 4% for Fe3O4/TiO2, and 5% for Fe3O4 in the next two intervals. This incident might be credited to the removal of organic matter residual up to the temperature of 800 °C, which is much more abundant occurred in the Fe3O4 sample. However, the GQDS sample is experienced a significant weight loss of up to 90% totally until 800 °C, indicating the destruction of age state functional groups on the GQDS nanosheets surface. In the case of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS, TGA graph patterns display three slope peaks at around 200, 500, and 575 °C. The GQDS sample displays about 8% weight loss beyond the temperature of 575 °C. This weight reduction is ascribed to the destruction of GQDS covering the surface of GQDS/Fe3O4/TiO2. It can be concluded that the loading of GQDS on magnetic anatase TiO2 was successful due to the weight loss of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS.

The BJH pore size distribution results and the BET adsorption/desorption isotherms of Fe3O4, TiO2, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS are presented in Fig. 2c. According to IUPAC classification, the isotherm curve of TiO2, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS adsorption/desorption is the type V and hysteresis loop of type H1 confirm the presence of a large number of mesopores [28]. It can be clear that the H3 loops of Fe3O4/TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS sorbents are of a composite in nature. The BJH plots show the pore size of the as-synthesized nanocomposite materials [22] which the pore sizes are found to be about 10.62, 4.62, 1.22, 6.91, and 6.91 nm for bare TiO2, Fe3O4, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS, respectively. According to IUPAC adsorption isotherms classification, the bare TiO2 nanoparticles and the as-synthesized composites (Fe3O4/TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS) lie in the range of mesoporous materials (pore size range between 2 and 50 nm) [29]. As demonstrated in Table 1 , the measured surface areas of Fe3O4, TiO2, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS were about 65.78, 35.07, 4.52, 43.68, and 38.00 m2/g, respectively. As indicated in inside Fig. 2c, the extensive physisorption isotherm obtained on isotherms GQDS is completely reversible and the entire adsorption branch of the H3 loop seems to be a type III isotherm. The adsorption isotherms of Fe3O4, TiO2, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS samples display low slope region related with multilayer adsorption on pore structure, followed by pore condensation in mesopores region at the relative pressure range between 0.75 and 0.95P/Po, which is appeared by the characteristic H1 hysteresis loop, and ended with a plateau region, at the relative pressure of 0.99P/Po, representing filled mesopores. As indicated in Fig. 2c and Table 1, by considering the identification of the isotherms type V, H1 hysteresis loops and mesopore size analysis of the BJH method, we note very good agreement between the data obtained from the adsorption/desorption branches of the isotherms for Fe3O4, TiO2, GQDS, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS samples. Subsequently, the aforementioned statements confirm the successful fabrication of mesoporous Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites. On the other hand, Fe3O4 and TiO2 nanoparticles loaded probably on the GQDS nanosheets surface, which led to decreasing the surface area and porosity of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalyst.

FT-IR results were performed to confirm the interaction between materials. Fig. 3 a displays the result of vibrational modes of surface functional groups in GQDS. The strongest peak occurs at 1585 cm−1 that is correlated to the mode of CC stretching. Other absorption peaks display the presence of CH (1376 cm−1 and 2976 cm−1), CO (1206 cm−1), and OH (3400 cm−1) peaks of H2O. Fig. 3a reveals the result of Fe3O4 with a vibration mode at 632 cm−1 associated with the FeO bonds. Fig. 3a illustrates the result of the TiO2 spectrum with three vibration mods at 3370, 1630, and 650 cm−1 ascribed to stretching modes of and hydroxyl group, bending modes of hydrogen-bonded water molecules (or OH groups) of and stretching frequency of TiOTi bridging, respectively [30]. FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4/TiO2shows (Fig. 3a), Fe3O4 peak overlaps with the TiOTi stretching vibrational mode which corresponded to the TiO2 layer. Moreover, the peak intensities located at 703 and 784 cm−1 confirm the stretching modes of the TiOTi in the anatase TiO2 phase [10]. Fig. 3a displays FT-IR spectra of the final product including Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS with numerous vibrational bands, due to CC (1585 cm−1), CH (1376 cm−1 and 2976 cm−1), CO (1206 cm−1), and OH (3400 cm−1), TiOC (650 cm−1) and FeO (592 cm−1). It proves the nanocomposite of TiO2/Fe3O4/GQDS forms well. Those of the new weak absorption bands that appeared at 1450 and 1170 cm−1 might be related to the formation bond of FeO between the iron in Fe3O4 and carboxyl groups in GQDS structures [17].

XPS analysis was applied to explore the surface chemistry analysis of Fe3O4/TiO2/QDG, as displayed in Fig. 3b-f. The total XPS spectrum of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS (Fig. 3b) presents two strong peaks positioned around 284.6 eV and 530.2 eV are ascribed to C1s and O1s signals, respectively, a strong peak positioned at 458.9 eV is attributed to the Ti2p and the other two weak bands appeared at the 39.4 and 564.3 eV are ascribed to the Ti3p and the Ti2s, respectively, and bonding energy value of 711.1 eV is attributed to Fe2p, indicated to the oxidized surface of Fe3O4, and a weak peak related to Fe3p is shown at a separation energy value of around 61.7 eV. The atomic weight% of carbon, oxygen, iron, and titanium was 23.57, 49.82, 17.12, and 9.49%, respectively. The binding energy region of C1s XPS spectra is presented in Fig. 3c. The chemical composition of the surface exhibited two strong CC and CC bonds (graphitic groups) positioned at 284.8 eV, which are corresponding to sp2 carbon. In Fig. 3c, the spectra of COTi, COH (hydroxyl and carboxylic groups), COC, and CO (carbonyl) are positioned at 283.5, 285.1, 286.7, and 288.8 eV, respectively, which are conforming sp3 carbon and are verified by the FT-IR spectra. The XPS spectra of the O 1s binding energy region showed three signals at 530.2, 531.5, and 532.6 eV, indicating TiO, CO, OH groups, respectively, which is supported by the observations of FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3d). The functional groups of carboxylic acid (COOH) of GQDs and the surface hydroxyl group of Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticles will form the TiOC bonds of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposites during the hydrothermal process. These results confirmed that functionalized GQDs structure with oxidized surface functional groups formed during the synthesis. Furthermore, the incidence of graphitic sp3 carbon in the XPS spectra confirms that the planarity GQDs structure reduced and defects appeared in the as-prepared GQDs, indicating that carbon was the main component of the GQDs. The slightly negative and positive shifts in the separation energy values could be related to the partial oxidation states of Fe3O4 and can be due to a change in the chemical bonding of an element [11]. These results reveal that TiO2 and Fe3O4 existed possibly as distinct phases in the Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst, which is consistent with our previous report [10]. In this work, the Fe3O4 spectra were analyzed to estimate the ferrous to the ferric ratio on the surface. Fig. 3e shows the XPS 2p core line spectra of Fe atoms in the oxide form of Fe3O4, indicating that the core line separation energies for Fe2p1/2 and Fe2p3/2 are positioned at about 724.1 and 710.8 eV, respectively. This is seen from Fig. 3e, these spectra are contributed to the binding energies of ferrous and ferric, respectively, and the peak intensity ratio of Fe2+ to Fe3+ is observed of about 0.66. The relative amounts of Fe2O3 of about 39.8% and Fe3O4 of about 60.2% were found, indicating the dominance of the Fe3O4 spectra component. Also, it is observed that Fe2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks had two satellite signals, as displayed in Fig. 3e. The Fe 2P1/2 peaks at bonding energy of 726.3 eV with two satellite peaks of 730.2 and 733.7 eV matched to the oxidation state of Fe3+, the peak of Fe2p3/2 located at around f 713 eV with two satellite signals of 716.9 and 720.4 eV were attributed to the oxidation state of Fe2+ in iron oxide nanoparticles. The XPS results confirm the fabrication of two composites of magnetite and the findings in the current report was in accordance with the earlier findings [31]. Fig. 3f displays the two XPS spectra Ti2p peaks positioned at the bonding energies of 458.8 and 464.5 eV, confirming the fabrication of the anatase layer of TiO2. The surface chemical stoichiometry of Ti2p showed two strong peaks for anatase of TiO2 crystals at binding energies of 458.8 eV (Ti3+) and 464.5 eV (Ti4+), which corresponded to two chemical levels from doublet fitting with the XPS Ti2p3/2 and the Ti2p1/2 signals. The existence of TiO2 (Ti4+) oxide layer on Fe3O4/TiO2 is more obvious than Ti2O3 (Ti3+), which indicates Ti4+ characteristic of tetrahedral coordination with oxygen-rich in TiO2. The high resolution of the XPS spectra proves the oxidation state of Ti4+ on the surface. The peak position at 5.7 eV separation energy of the Ti2p peaks is proportional to the energy reported for TiO2 anatase. The present results are consistent with the earlier findings [32].

FESEM images of nanoparticles expose the nearly spherical morphology of as-synthesized material with agglomeration for Fe3O4, TiO2, Fe3O4/TiO2, and entirely for GQDS (Fig. 1, MethodsX). The size of TiO2 particles measured by FESEM was from 49 to 118 nm for composite materials and below 2 µm for GQDS because of agglomeration while FESEM may not be suited well for particle size measurement. Consequently, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis is used to provide accurate two-dimension nanoparticle sizes, due to number-based size distributions. TEM analyses were used to indicate the morphology of Fe3O4, TiO2, GQDs, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS composite nanoparticles (Fig. 1, MethodsX). Fig. 1a (MethodsX) shows Fe3O4 nanoparticles in a spherical shape with a range from 10 to 20 nm dimensions. However, agglomeration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles may correspond to the high surface energy of the nanomaterials [33]. Fig. 1b (MethodsX) displays the TEM images of TiO2 nanoparticles with spherical clusters and mostly with a size smaller than 30 nm. Fig. 1c (MethodsX) presents the TEM image of the GQDS which is spherical and shows almost particle size of about 3–8 nm. Fig. 1d (MethodsX) represents a TEM image of Fe3O4/TiO2, demonstrating that the fine particles of Fe3O4 are covered randomly on the surface of TiO2. Correspondingly, the agglomeration pattern of magnetic particles was stated by Dewi et al. [8]. The TEM image of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposite presents in Fig. 1e (MethodsX). This image shows the particle diameter of mainly about 50 nm that are faceted with nanostructure faces. By a deep inspection of inset images of EDX mapping (Fig. 2, MethodsX), one can perceive the identical distribution of elemental composition on the surface of the as-synthesized materials. The FESEM image of the as-prepared Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposite is illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Besides, HRTEM analyses were used to examine surface morphology and real sizes of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS composite nanoparticles (Fig. 4). Based on the FESEM morphology analysis of nanocomposites obtained by coating magnetite crystalline TiO2 decorated GQDs, spherical particles and sheets morphology with irregular morphology and size about less 100 nm were witnessed by FESEM (Fig. 4a). Additionally, HRTEM image of Fig. 4b confirms the formation of spherical and composite nanoparticles with size less than 100 nm. However, Based on the morphological observation from HRTEM analysis (Fig. 4c), Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposites have an outer porous wall and clear lattice fringes consisting of magnetic TiO2 decorated with GQDs well-defined graphene lattice morphologies inside the particles. Fig. 4d demonstrates the lattice planes with a lattice distance of 0.350 nm that relates to the inter-planar spacing of anatase TiO2 for the (101) plane, while the lattice spacing of 0.299 nm indicates the in-plane lattice part of GQDs [34]. Also, the SEM/EDX instrument was analyzed surface elemental analysis and their corresponding elemental composition is represented in Table 2 . Conversely, SEM/EDX detected major peaks of Fe and O, and Ti and O, C and O and Fe, Ti, C, O in the Fe3O4, TiO2, Fe3O4/TiO2, Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS structures, respectively. On the other hand, the EDX detected no other significant peak impurities in the Fe3O4, TiO2, Fe3O4/TiO2 chemical structures (Fig. 2a-d, MethodsX), suggesting iron oxides and titanium oxides were the major inorganic constituent of the nanoparticle structure. Nevertheless, the intense peak at 2 eV is related to gold when a gold coating sample is applied before analysis, while the peak at 5 eV is ascribed to the aluminum foil used for sample holding during analysis. After GQDS coating on the Fe3O4/TiO2 surface nanocomposites, the elemental compositions were changed and detected the existence of main element Ti, Fe, O, and especially C in the structure, proving the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS nanocomposite photocatalyst (Table 2). Also, the EDX mappings provide evidence for the uniform and precise nanoscale distribution of Fe3O4, TiO2, and GQDS onto the structure of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS particles (inset Fig. 2, MethodsX). This figure indicates that the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalyst was successfully synthesized.

The assessment of magnetic properties and magnetic fields (MH) tracer of the as-prepared Fe3O4, Fe3O4/TiO2, and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS samples were performed at ambient temperature using VSM. The magnetization-hysteresis curve of Fe3O4 nanoparticles verifies the superparamagnetic behavior and determines a saturation magnetization 60.41 emu/g, as shown in Fig. 5 . A nonlinear manner with a negligible magnetic hysteresis loop was obtained at room temperature (Figure not shown). This is to be emphasized that Fe3O4/TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS samples decorated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles anticipated having comparable magnetic properties attributable to the same preparation conditions applied for both specimens. The MH curves show the magnetic characteristics of Fe3O4/TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalysts, indicating that these composite photocatalysts remain magnetic after coating [35]. A magnetization saturation value of Fe3O4/TiO2 was about 1.70 emu/g while that of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalyst was 1.16 emu/g. The decrease in saturation magnetization of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS is mostly related to the presence of nonmagnetic TiO2 and GQDS shells on the surface of Fe3O4. It is worth taking into consideration that the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS sample contains 12.5% of Fe3O4 nanoparticle. However, the saturation value of magnetization of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalyst was sufficient to be collected from the solution by using an external magnetic field in a short observation time and had excellent dispensability in water by slightly handshaking after taking away the external magnetic field. The photograph of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS dispersed in water solution is presented in the top-left inset of Fig. 5b and that removed from the solution with the help of an external magnetic field are presented in the downright inset of Fig. 5b.

Fig. 6 shows the bandgap energy (Eg) measurement from the Tauc method and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the graphene quantum dots (GQDS) and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS. The inset Fig. 6 shows two clear peaks near 216 and 263 nm related to GQDs of π → π* transition of sp2C hybridization of aromatic CC bonds, which confirm the reported GQDS [36]. Comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the GQDS and nanocomposite samples show the same peak band, indicating GQDS particle loaded on the surface of Fe3O4/TiO2. However, comparing the spectra of the GQDS system and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS samples reveals the amendment differences. Then, the linear section of the Tauc plot is extrapolated to (αhν)2 = 0 and the direct bandgap energies of the GQDS and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS were extracted, as exhibited in Fig. 6. The bandgap energy of the GQDS sample is 1.77 eV. After the coating process, the bandgap energy of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS sample was shifted to 2.56 eV as a result of the agglomeration of the nanocomposite into larger crystallites. In the case of anatase TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2, the values of band gaps have been reported to 3.15 (TiO2), and 2.71 eV (FT0.125), respectively [9].

The various photocatalysts samples demonstrated in Fig. 7 were explored for the photocatalytic activity of methylene blue (MB) oxidation under UVA light radiation. These figures illustrate the conforming nonlinear relationship between C t and exposure time, indicating that the kinetic model fits well with the apparent first-order reaction for the MB photodegradation. The corresponding data of percentage removals, photodegradation rate constants (k 1), and determination coefficient (R 2) are given in Table 3 . The average percentage removal and standard deviation (SD) are computed for each variable for the three replicate measurements of the MB dye removal.

Fig. 7a shows the photocatalytic activity of Fe3O4 and anatase TiO2 nanoparticles and different weight ratios of Fe3O4 loaded onto the surface of TiO2 (FT0.125, FT0.25, FT0.5, and FT1) for MB photodegradation. The experiments were carried out at experimental conditions of photocatalyst dose 500 mg/L, pH 9, dye concentration 10 mg/L, and temperature of 30 °C. The photocatalytic activity for MB decomposition was tested under both dark conditions and light exposure of UVA irradiation. In all experimental conditions, results determined negligible removal under the dark condition that was due to adsorption characteristics of nanoparticles. The basic photocatalytic degradation of MB via TiO2 can be summarized with the following reaction mechanism [37]:

In the aforementioned mechanisms, the electron-hole recombination determines the key rate-limiting step in the MB photooxidation process by anatase TiO2 photocatalyst. Under these conditions, the consumption of superoxide radical anion (O2
−) and hydroxyl radicals can mineralize MB dye. Table 3 shows that the as-synthesized anatase TiO2 in this work (i.e., without any Fe3O4) shows a comparable photocatalytic process related to Fe3O4/TiO2. The removal efficiency falls to 72.63 ± 2.81% for pure TiO2 compared with 75.47 ± 3.13% obtained by applying FT0.125. With a decreased loading of Fe3O4, the photocatalytic activity increases, as seen in Fig. 7a. By detailed scrutiny of Table 3, a better photocatalytic activity can be obtained, if one compares the values of rate constants with a decrease of Fe3O4 weight ratio. Under UVA light irradiation (Fig. 7a), a very negligible amount of MB removal occurred in the existence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (i.e., without any TiO2). In the case of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, when Fe3O4 particles absorbed a photon with hν energy, photoexcited electrons are shifted from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), releasing a hole (ecb − and hvb + pairs) backward. The main competing Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions involve in ecb − and hvb + annihilation reaction, as follows [38]:

Fig. 7b shows the photocatalytic activity of different amounts of nanocomposite dose ranging from 100 to 500 mg/L for MB photodegradation, while the other variables were kept constant at MB concentration10 mg/L, pH 9, and temperature 30 °C under dark and UVA irradiation to achieve an optimum dose of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS photocatalyst for MB mineralization. The maximum MB removal of about 88.82 ± 2.56% was obtained at 500 mg/100 mL of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS. However, percentage removal increased from 51.41 ± 1.92% to about 100% with an increase of the photocatalyst dose from 100 to 1000 mg/mL and then decreased (data not shown). With an increase in photocatalyst dose, UVA light absorption was enhanced, which improved the photodegradation rate [39]. The decrease in removal efficiency at the higher photocatalyst dose was due to deactivation of active sites in a suspension via collision within nanocomposites particles and also increasing the turbidity of the photocatalyst suspension and UVA radiation, preventing the light penetration as well as photocatalyst aggregation (particle–particle interaction) which causes a reduction in effective surface area of photocatalyst [10].

Fig. 7c shows the effect of various pH ranges from 3 to 11 on the photodegradation progress. The experiments were carried out at dye concentrations 10 mg/L, photocatalyst dose 400 mg/L, and temperature 30 °C. It is obvious that aqueous pH affects the surface charge and agglomeration size of the photocatalyst in aqueous media. However, photocatalytic degradation reactions occur in acid and alkaline condition as [40]:

A sequential reaction happens in the presence of DO under acid and alkaline conditions. Moreover, the pH solution influences the photocatalytic efficiency and hydrophobicity, solubility, and speciation behaviors of pollutants. In light of the aforementioned, the rate of photodegradation reaction of cation pollutants increases with increasing pH in an aqueous media, because of the adsorption behavior of MB dye on the surface of photocatalyst. According to Fig. 8 , the pH of the point of zero charges (pHPZC) for Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS was about 5.85, specifying that the photocatalyst surface has a negative charge in an alkaline pH (or pH > 5.85) [41] and a positive charge at moderate acidic media (or pH < 5.85). Whereas, the adsorption of a cationic dye seems to occur at a pH > pHzpc of the Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite. According to the literature, the pHPZC of TiO2 is reported to be at 6.8 [42], therefore the as-synthesized nanocomposite shows the electron-hole recombination separation through the presence of GQDs and Fe3O4 to develop transfer of the electron from the CB of Fe3O4/GQDs to the CB of TiO2 [42]. Therefore, an increase in pH increases the number of OH− ions which makes the TiO2 surface negatively charge. Hence, the adsorption of the cationic form of dye increases because of the magnitude of the electrostatic force of attraction between the negative surface charge of TiO2 nanocomposite and the positively charged of cationic dye at the basic pH. This causes attraction between a positively charged dye and negatively charged anatase Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs. We found that MB removal efficiency was enhanced by increasing pH, which suggests that the TiO2-MB interaction is purely electrostatic. The predicted apparent first-order kinetic model was shown in good agreement with the test observed results as evident from the high correlation coefficient (R 2) values given in Table 3. The rate constants of photocatalytic oxidation of 0.87, 0.8, 0.4, 0.3 and 0.2 min−1 were obtained for pH3, pH5, pH7, pH9 and pH11, respectively. As given in Table 3, the maximum removal of 86.08 ± 4.26% was achieved at pH11, which explains the direct oxidative decomposition through the photogenerated holes [43]. Fig. 7c shows that the performance removal at pH 11 was better than pH 9. This can be explained by the fact that the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite for MB dye might be increased when the solution pH was increased. This is to be emphasized that the cationic form of MB is the only species present at pH 11. Therefore, enough negative charge on the surface of the nanocomposite was more favorable to adsorb the cationic form of MB at pH 11, lead to enhance the electrostatic attraction between the cation form of MB and Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs and resulting in an increase in dye removal. However, this study is inconsistent with what has been found previously [44]. They have demonstrated that the synthesized amine-functionalized GQDs/TiO2 exhibited greater photocatalytic oxidation of methyl orange (an anionic dye) than TiO2 alone under visible light radiation.

The degradation of photocatalytic activity of MB dye by various Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite comprising the various amount of GQDs (wt%) on Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticles under UVA light is shown in Fig. 4 (MethodsX). With an optimum loading amount of Fe3O4/TiO2 to GQDs (1: 0.1 wt%), Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs reveals a removal efficiency of about 15.4 and 47.1% higher than those of bare TiO2 and GQDs, respectively, which is a significantly more enhancement than the bare anatase TiO2 and Fe3O4/TiO2 nanoparticle photocatalytic systems. Therefore, photogenerated electron-hole pairs of Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs heterogeneous nanocomposites can absorb more O2 than bare anatase TiO2. This means that the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs creates a separation of photo-induced electron/hole pairs [45] in Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposites to facilitate photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. Based on the above discussion, we conclude that the existence of GQDs on the surface of Fe3O4/TiO2 was increased the photocatalytic activity of anatase TiO2.

Fig. 7d demonstrates the influence of various dye concentrations ranging from 5 to 20 mg/L as an important parameter on removal efficiency, while the other variables were kept constant. As given in Table 3, the degradation rate constant decreased with rising dye content that could be because of an increase of adsorption of MB molecules on the photocatalyst surface that prevents direct contact with the hydroxyl radicals (HO
) or VB holes. On the other hand, the reaction between MB and HO
may be limited by the mass transfer phenomenon, indicating that increasing the concentration of MB increases reserved effects. However, photogenerated holes take part in the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of MB in the existence of a HO
scavenger. Another cause may be the absorption of UV light by MB molecules and decrease HO
and HO2
concentrations [42]. Results showed that removal efficiencies of about 85.73 ± 4.55, 83.21 ± 4.40, 80.40 ± 3.64, and 75.58 ± 3.62% were obtained for MB concentration of 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg/L, respectively. However, other studies found similar results and one reported that the MB degradation of about 75% under UV irradiation using Fe-CNT/TiO2 composite photocatalysts was obtained [46]. Furthermore, compared with TiO2 (P25), the photodegradation of MB using TiO2 (P25)/NQD was enhanced from 68% to 91% under UV irradiation [12]. Another example is the work of Dewi et al. [8] that the Fe3O4/SiO2/TiO2 composite showed to degrade 10 mg/L MB up to 91% under UV light for 1 h.

The present work confirmed that the photocatalysis process with GQDS anatase Fe3O4/TiO2 nanocomposites can be developed successfully for the degradation of cationic MB dye under different operational conditions. TEM, EDX-FESEM, FT-IR, XRD, and TGA analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of nanocomposite particles. The obtained photocatalyst had a crystal size of 19.84 nm with a saturation magnetization of 1.16 emu/g. We examined experiments in different conditions of photocatalyst dose, pH, and MB concentration for achieving the optimum condition of photocatalytic oxidation of MB under UVA light radiation. Experimental findings showed the photocatalyst is very efficient for MB degradation and the apparent first-order reaction kinetics well explained the photocatalytic oxidation behavior. The predominant mechanism was mostly the direct oxidative memorization through the photogenerated holes. Future research will study elevating the regeneration process for the used Fe3O4/TiO2/GQDS, and validating its photocatalytic activity for the municipal/industrial wastewater treatment under visible light radiation.
